schools.summary: Paul opens by asserting his apostleship — 'an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God' — not self-appointed but commissioned by God's own will and authority; the letter addressed 'to the saints (the holy ones, set apart to God) which are at Ephesus' and 'to the faithful (believing, trustworthy) in Christ Jesus' — the believers there, marked by holiness and faith (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).
Calvin (with Tertullian): the title 'saints' belongs to all true believers, set apart and sanctified in Christ; Paul writes not to officials but to the whole holy and faithful body.

themes: Paul, an apostle of Jesus Christ by the will of God
to the saints which are at Ephesus, and to the faithful in Christ Jesus
the apostolic greeting
the saints and faithful in Christ

schools.summary: Paul's customary greeting: 'grace (free favour) be to you, and peace (the peace that flows from grace)' — the twofold blessing that opens his epistles, grace the source and peace the fruit; coming jointly 'from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ' — Father and Son named together as the one fountain of blessing, an incidental witness to Christ's deity (Barnes, Ellicott).

themes: Grace be to you, and peace, from God our Father, and from the Lord Jesus Christ
the apostolic blessing of grace and peace
grace the source, peace the fruit
Father and Son the one fountain

schools.summary: Paul bursts into a doxology that opens the great hymn of blessing (vv.3-14): 'blessed be (worthy of praise) the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ' — the source of all blessing is himself to be blessed; God 'hath blessed us with all spiritual blessings (every blessing of the Spirit, every gift of grace)' — the comprehensive bestowal of every spiritual good; 'in heavenly places (the heavenly realm, of a heavenly nature and destiny) in Christ' — flowing from and held in union with Christ (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Henry): the blessings are heavenly in nature and destiny, and held 'in Christ,' who is their source and channel; all the good the believer has flows from union with him.

themes: Blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ
who hath blessed us with all spiritual blessings in heavenly places in Christ
the doxology for all spiritual blessings
blessings heavenly and in Christ

schools.summary: God chose us 'in him (in Christ) before the foundation of the world' — an eternal purpose, not an afterthought or work of chance, formed before creation; the word 'chosen' (exelexato) denotes a definite selection with favour, referring (Barnes) to individuals, not merely nations, since a nation is but a collection of individuals and the same objection lies against electing nations as individuals; what God actually does he always meant to do. The design: 'that we should be holy and without blame before him' — holiness the fruit and goal of election, not its cause; the doctrine tends not to license sin but to produce holiness (Barnes, Robertson, JFB).

Calvin: the eternal election is the foundation and first cause of our calling and all blessings; the very time (before the world was made) proves it free, for we could have deserved nothing — all were lost in Adam, so nothing worthy could be foreseen (Romans 9:11). 'In him' excludes all merit; holiness, purity, and every excellence are the fruit of election, not its cause; predestination, handled soberly, magnifies God's mercy and stops the mouth of human boasting.

Wesley: God chose 'both Jews and Gentiles, whom he foreknew as believing in Christ' (1 Peter 1:2) — election conditioned on foreseen faith. Clarke: takes it of God's eternal purpose to bring the Gentiles (as a people) into gospel privileges, as he once chose Israel — a corporate election to privilege, the phrase echoing Israel's settlement in the promised land.

debates.question: On what ground are believers 'chosen before the foundation of the world'?

sides.position: Unconditional individual election founded on God's free will alone, not foreseen merit (all were lost in Adam)
Election conditioned on foreseen faith — God chose those he foreknew as believing
A corporate election of the Gentiles into gospel privilege, as Israel was once chosen

themes: he hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world
that we should be holy and without blame before him in love
the eternal election of believers in Christ
holiness the fruit of election

schools.summary: God, having chosen, 'predestinated (proorizo — determined beforehand, set bounds before) us unto the adoption of children (huiothesia, sonship) by Jesus Christ' — foreordaining that believers should be brought, through Christ's mediation, into the dignity and rights of sons of God, 'to himself'; 'according to the good pleasure (eudokia — sovereign purpose) of his will' — the evident object being to state why God chose the heirs of salvation: as seemed good to him, not from any control or merit of man (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the cause is God's free good pleasure alone — he did not seek a cause outside himself, but predestinated us because such was his will; the phrase expressly sets aside all merit, free-will, and fancied preparations, that the whole glory of salvation be God's.
Wesley: God's free, fixed, unalterable purpose to confer the dignity of sonship and joint-heirship with Christ on all those who should believe in Christ, and those only.

themes: Having predestinated us unto the adoption of children by Jesus Christ to himself
according to the good pleasure of his will
predestination to adoption
the good pleasure of God's will

schools.summary: The ultimate end of election and predestination: 'to the praise of the glory of his grace' — that God's grace might be praised and glorified, the highest design of all; 'wherein he hath made us accepted (echaritosen — highly favoured, made acceptable, the word used of Mary in Luke 1:28) in the beloved (Christ, the Beloved Son)' — believers are graciously accepted by God in union with his beloved Son (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril: it is 'in the beloved' — in Christ — that we find acceptance; God's favour reaches us only through and in his Son, the well-beloved in whom we are reconciled.

themes: To the praise of the glory of his grace
he hath made us accepted in the beloved
the praise of God's grace
acceptance in the Beloved

schools.summary: 'In whom (in Christ) we have redemption (apolytrosis — deliverance by ransom, buying back) through his blood' — believers are redeemed, set free from sin's bondage and penalty, by the price of Christ's blood, his atoning death; this redemption consisting in 'the forgiveness (remission) of sins' — the cancelling of guilt; and 'according to the riches (the abundance, the wealth) of his grace' — not grudging or scanty but flowing from the boundless wealth of God's grace (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): forgiveness flows from the riches of grace; the redemption is wholly of God's abundant favour, the blood of Christ the ransom price by which guilt is remitted.

themes: In whom we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of sins
according to the riches of his grace
redemption and forgiveness through Christ's blood
the riches of grace

schools.summary: The grace just mentioned is that 'wherein he hath abounded (made to overflow, lavished) toward us' — God's grace has been poured out abundantly upon believers, not given sparingly; bestowed 'in all wisdom and prudence (phronesis — intelligence, understanding)' — either the wisdom God displayed in the plan of redemption, or the wisdom and understanding he imparts to believers; God's grace is a wise and intelligent grace, linked to the revealing of the mystery of his will (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: he hath abounded toward us in all wisdom and prudence
God's grace abounding in all wisdom and prudence
grace lavished abundantly
a wise and intelligent grace

schools.summary: God, in his abounding grace, has 'made known unto us the mystery (mysterion — a secret once hidden, now revealed) of his will' — disclosing his hidden saving purpose, which human wisdom could never have discovered; the mystery being God's plan of redemption (especially the uniting of all things in Christ and the inclusion of the Gentiles), formerly hidden, now revealed in the gospel (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: the revelation flows from God's 'good pleasure, which he purposed in himself' — his own sovereign, free counsel, not prompted from outside; the mystery springs from his own will alone.

themes: Having made known unto us the mystery of his will
according to his good pleasure which he hath purposed in himself
God making known the mystery of his will
the revealed saving purpose

schools.summary: The mystery is to be accomplished 'in the dispensation (oikonomia — administration, ordering) of the fulness of times' — the era of the gospel, the appointed time when God's plan comes to its head in Christ; God's purpose to 'gather together in one (anakephalaiosasthai — sum up, head up, unite under one head) all things in Christ, both which are in heaven, and which are on earth' — to bring all things to unity and order under Christ as their head (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the things in heaven and on earth — the whole universe, angels and men, the divided creation — are to be reconciled and united under Christ; the cosmic reach of redemption, all summed up in him.

themes: in the dispensation of the fulness of times he might gather together in one all things in Christ
both which are in heaven, and which are on earth
the gathering of all things in Christ
the cosmic consummation under Christ

schools.summary: 'In whom (in Christ) also we have obtained an inheritance (eklerothemen — were made a heritage, or obtained a lot/portion)' — believers are given a share in the inheritance of God, made heirs in Christ; this inheritance 'being predestinated (foreordained) according to the purpose (prothesis — the settled design) of God' — the heirship flows from God's eternal plan (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

Calvin: God 'worketh all things after the counsel of his own will' — a strong assertion of God's universal providence and sovereign freedom; he accomplishes all according to his own wise counsel, the inheritance secured by his unchangeable purpose.

themes: In whom also we have obtained an inheritance, being predestinated according to the purpose
of him who worketh all things after the counsel of his own will
the inheritance obtained by predestination
God working all things after his own counsel

schools.summary: The design of the inheritance and predestination: 'that we should be to the praise of his glory' — that believers, made heirs, should result in the praise and glory of God (his glory the end of all, the refrain of vv.6, 12, 14); 'who first trusted (or first hoped — proelpikotas) in Christ' probably refers to the Jewish believers (Paul and others) who were the first to hope in the Messiah, before the Gentiles came in (v.13), though some take it of all believers as those who set their hope in Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: That we should be to the praise of his glory, who first trusted in Christ
the design that believers be to God's praise
the first to hope in Christ
the refrain of God's glory

schools.summary: The order of salvation for the Gentiles: they 'heard the word of truth (the gospel of your salvation),' and then 'trusted (believed)' in Christ — hearing the gospel preceding and producing faith; and upon believing they 'were sealed (esphragisthete — marked, secured, confirmed) with that holy Spirit of promise' — the Holy Spirit, promised to believers, given as a seal: a mark of ownership, a confirmation of genuineness, and a pledge of security (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the Spirit is the promised gift of the Father (Joel 2, Acts 2), the seal that marks believers as God's own and certifies their belonging to him.

themes: after that ye heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation
after that ye believed, ye were sealed with that holy Spirit of promise
the Gentiles hearing, believing, and sealed
the sealing of the Spirit

schools.summary: The sealing Spirit 'is the earnest (arrabon — a down-payment, first instalment, pledge guaranteeing the rest) of our inheritance' — the gift of the Spirit now is the first instalment and guarantee of the full inheritance to come; given 'until the redemption of the purchased possession' — until the full and final redemption (of the body, the complete deliverance) of those whom God has purchased as his own; 'unto the praise of his glory' (the third doxological refrain, Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the Spirit secures believers as God's purchased possession until the final redemption; the earnest is the certain pledge that God will complete what he has begun, to the praise of his glory.

themes: Which is the earnest of our inheritance until the redemption of the purchased possession
unto the praise of his glory
the Spirit as the earnest of the inheritance
the pledge of full redemption

schools.summary: Paul, having recounted the blessings of grace, now turns to prayer: 'wherefore I also, after I heard of your faith in the Lord Jesus, and love unto all the saints' — the report of their faith and love prompts his thanksgiving and prayer; the pairing of 'faith in the Lord Jesus' (toward Christ) and 'love unto all the saints' (toward the brethren) being the twin marks of a true Christian, faith Godward and love manward (Barnes, Henry, Chrysostom).
Calvin: where there is true faith in Christ, there follows love to all the saints; the two are inseparable, and their report gladdens the apostle and moves him to prayer.

themes: after I heard of your faith in the Lord Jesus, and love unto all the saints
Paul's hearing of their faith and love
faith Godward and love manward
the twin marks of a Christian

schools.summary: Paul's habit: he 'ceases not to give thanks for you' — his thanksgiving for them is unceasing, a continual gratitude to God for their faith and love; and he also remembers them in prayer, 'making mention of you in my prayers' — naming them before God, interceding for their further growth (as the prayer of vv.17-19 shows); gratitude and petition go together (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: Cease not to give thanks for you, making mention of you in my prayers
Paul's ceaseless thanksgiving and prayer
gratitude and intercession joined
the apostle's praying heart

schools.summary: Paul addresses his prayer to 'the God of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Father of glory' — the glorious Father, the source of all glory, to whom Christ in his humanity refers as his God; his petition that God 'give unto you the spirit (the disposition, or the Holy Spirit working) of wisdom and revelation' — spiritual wisdom and the revealing of divine things — 'in the knowledge of him (God)' — the deep, experiential knowledge of God himself the goal of the gift (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the wisdom and revelation aim at the true knowledge of God; the goal of the Spirit's gift is a fuller, deeper knowing of God himself, not mere information.

themes: the Father of glory, may give unto you the spirit of wisdom and revelation
in the knowledge of him
the prayer for the spirit of wisdom
the deep knowledge of God

schools.summary: Paul prays that 'the eyes of your understanding (kardias — heart) being enlightened' — that the inward eyes of the soul may be opened and illumined by the Spirit, to perceive spiritual things; that they may know 'what is the hope of his calling' (the glorious hope to which God's call summons them) and 'what the riches of the glory of his inheritance in the saints' (the surpassing wealth and glory of the inheritance God has in or for his saints; either the glory believers will inherit, or the glory God finds in possessing his people, Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril: the enlightened heart perceives the hope and the glory of the inheritance; spiritual knowledge is the gift of the Spirit, opening the eyes of the soul to see what flesh cannot.

themes: The eyes of your understanding being enlightened
what is the hope of his calling, and what the riches of the glory of his inheritance in the saints
the prayer for enlightened eyes
the hope and the inheritance

schools.summary: The third thing Paul prays they may know: 'the exceeding greatness (the surpassing, immeasurable greatness) of his power to us-ward who believe' — the immense power of God exerted toward and in believers; 'according to the working (energeian — the energetic operation) of his mighty power (the strength of his might)' — Paul heaps up words for power to express its immensity, the same power that raised Christ (v.20) now working in believers (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril (with Calvin): the power that saves, keeps, and will raise believers is nothing less than the almighty power of God — the greatest assurance of their hope and salvation.

themes: what is the exceeding greatness of his power to us-ward who believe
according to the working of his mighty power
the prayer to know God's power toward believers
almighty power the believer's assurance

schools.summary: The measure of God's power is the power he 'wrought in Christ when he raised him from the dead' — the resurrection of Christ is the supreme display of God's might, the pattern and pledge of his power toward believers; and God 'set him at his own right hand in the heavenly places' — exalting the risen Christ to the place of supreme honour, authority, and dominion at God's right hand (Barnes, Robertson).

Cyril (with Calvin): the session at God's right hand marks Christ's universal sovereignty and his divine glory; the exalted Christ reigns over all, raised by the same power that will raise his people.

themes: which he wrought in Christ, when he raised him from the dead
set him at his own right hand in the heavenly places
God's power in raising and exalting Christ
the exalted Christ at God's right hand

schools.summary: Christ is exalted 'far above all principality, and power, and might, and dominion' — above every rank of angelic and created authority, the heaped-up terms denoting all orders of beings and powers, however exalted; above 'every name that is named' — every title of dignity, authority, or honour that exists or can be conceived; and this 'not only in this world, but also in that which is to come' — his supremacy extending through both ages (Barnes, Robertson).
Cyril: Christ's exaltation is eternal and universal, reaching through the present age and the age to come; no name in either world is above his.

themes: Far above all principality, and power, and might, and dominion, and every name that is named
not only in this world, but also in that which is to come
Christ exalted above all powers and every name
universal supremacy

schools.summary: God 'hath put all things under his feet' (echoing Psalm 8:6) — all things, without exception, are made subject to the exalted Christ, the whole creation beneath his dominion; and 'gave him to be the head over all things to the church' — Christ, supreme over all the universe, is given as head to the church in particular, his universal lordship exercised for the benefit of his body (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: all Christ's authority over all things is directed to the good of the church; the church's head rules the universe on her behalf, that all things serve her salvation.

themes: hath put all things under his feet
gave him to be the head over all things to the church
all things under Christ's feet
head over all to the church

schools.summary: The church 'is his body' — the living organism of which Christ is the head; believers are united to him as members to a body, sharing his life; 'the fulness (pleroma) of him that filleth all in all' — either the church is that which is filled by Christ (he fills her with his fulness), or, more strikingly, the church is in some sense the completion of Christ, the body without which the head would be incomplete; 'him that filleth all in all' is Christ, who fills the whole universe with himself (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: it is a high honour that Christ, who fills all things, should account the church his fulness — as though he were not complete without his members; such is his love and union with his body.

themes: Which is his body, the fulness of him that filleth all in all
the church as Christ's body and his fulness
union of head and members
Christ accounting the church his completion
